content.text: Job stories shift the focus from role-based feature descriptions to the situational motivations that drive user behaviour. For conversational AI, this shift is critical: the same user can be in radically different contexts at turn 1, turn 4, or after a misunderstanding, and each state demands a different system response.

attrs.alt: Job Story Anatomy for Conversational AI Flows

content.text: Job Stories vs User Stories for Conversational AI
User stories describe features from a role's perspective (
to check my order status). Job stories describe the situational context that triggers a need (
I have just been notified of a delay,
decide whether to cancel before dispatch).
Conversational AI makes job stories more essential than ever for three reasons:

The user's context changes turn by turn. A user asking 'what are my options?' means something different at turn 1 (exploring) vs. turn 6 (reviewing a shortlist) vs. after a correction ('I meant cheaper options').
Natural-language input rarely maps directly to a system action. Job stories force you to articulate the underlying motivation, which is the anchor for intent-classification design.
When a chatbot cannot handle a request, the failure mode is another conversational turn, not a 404 page. Job stories must include the graceful-degradation path as a first-class element.
When <situation>, I want to <outcome>, so I can <motivation>
Turn state, AI confidence, memory scope, failure path
Wrong turn, stale context, bad escalation
Anatomy of a Conversational Job Story
A well-formed job story for a conversational AI flow has five elements, not three.
The triggering context the user finds themselves in
Include turn number, prior actions, or emotional state where relevant
What the user is trying to accomplish in this interaction
Distinguish immediate desire from final goal — these often differ in AI flows
The underlying job-to-be-done that explains the desire
Surfaces the true acceptance criterion; the AI must serve this, not just the stated desire
Turn position, memory contents, confidence level, session history
Defines which slots are filled, what entities are in scope, and what the AI knows
What the AI should do when it cannot fulfil the story
Explicitly scoped so devs do not invent ad-hoc fallbacks mid-sprint

extensions are what separate a conversational job story from a standard one. Omitting them produces stories that only describe the happy path, leaving the three most common issues — misunderstanding, confidence failure, and stale context — untested until QA.
The following types encode a conversational job story for tooling and test-generation purposes.
Writing Job Stories by Turn State
Each turn state produces a distinct story class. A feature team should author at least one story per state for every major conversational task flow.
Entry stories describe what happens when the user arrives with no established context. The AI must handle maximum ambiguity.

ENTRY_STORY = ConversationalJobStory(
    story_id="JS-ORDER-001",
    situation=(
        "I have just opened the support chat immediately after receiving "
        "a shipping notification with no order details visible"
    ),
    outcome="ask about my order status without repeating information I already have",
    motivation="resolve my uncertainty quickly so I can plan my day",
    ai_context=AIContext(
        turn_state=TurnState.ENTRY,
        turn_index=0,
        slots=[
            SlotSpec("order_id",   required=True,  filled=False),
            SlotSpec("issue_type", required=True,  filled=False),
        ],
        confidence_min=0.0,
        memory_scope="none",
    ),
    fail_path=FailPath(
        trigger="User provides order ID not found in system",
        response="Acknowledge the error, ask user to verify the number, offer agent handoff",
        max_turns=2,
    ),
)
Mid-conversation stories fire after at least one successful turn. Context is available and the AI is expected to use it without asking the user to repeat themselves.

MID_STORY = ConversationalJobStory(
    story_id="JS-ORDER-002",
    situation=(
        "I have already told the assistant my order number and "
        "it has confirmed a delay; I am now asking about compensation"
    ),
    outcome="get compensation options without re-entering my order details",
    motivation="resolve the inconvenience efficiently and feel heard",
    ai_context=AIContext(
        turn_state=TurnState.MID,
        turn_index=3,
        slots=[
            SlotSpec("order_id",      required=True,  filled=True),
            SlotSpec("issue_type",    required=True,  filled=True),
            SlotSpec("compensation_preference", required=False, filled=False),
        ],
        confidence_min=0.75,
        memory_scope="session",
        active_entities=["ORD-88421", "delay_notification"],
    ),
    fail_path=FailPath(
        trigger="AI cannot determine compensation eligibility from context",
        response="Summarise what it knows, ask one targeted clarifying question",
        max_turns=1,
    ),
)
Correction stories are among the most commonly omitted. They test that the AI can surgically reset a single slot without discarding the rest of the conversation context.

CORRECTION_STORY = ConversationalJobStory(
    story_id="JS-ORDER-003",
    situation=(
        "I realise I gave the wrong order number two turns ago and "
        "want to correct just that detail without restarting the conversation"
    ),
    outcome="correct only the order ID so the rest of my context is preserved",
    motivation="avoid repeating myself and losing the issue description I already gave",
    ai_context=AIContext(
        turn_state=TurnState.CORRECTION,
        turn_index=4,
        slots=[
            SlotSpec("order_id",   required=True,  filled=True),  # being corrected
            SlotSpec("issue_type", required=True,  filled=True),  # must be preserved
        ],
        confidence_min=0.60,
        memory_scope="session",
    ),
    fail_path=FailPath(
        trigger="New order ID also not found",
        response="State clearly it cannot find either order; offer to transfer to an agent",
        max_turns=1,
    ),
)

A correction story's acceptance criterion must explicitly assert that non-corrected slots are unchanged after the repair turn. Without this assertion, implementations frequently wipe and re-ask all slots, creating the very frustration the user was trying to avoid.
Escalation stories ensure the AI knows when to stop trying. Session-end stories ensure partial work is not lost.

ESCALATION_STORY = ConversationalJobStory(
    story_id="JS-ORDER-004",
    situation=(
        "The assistant has not been able to resolve my refund question "
        "after two attempts and my frustration is evident"
    ),
    outcome="be transferred to a human agent who already has my conversation summary",
    motivation="not repeat myself a third time to a new person",
    ai_context=AIContext(
        turn_state=TurnState.ESCALATION,
        turn_index=6,
        slots=[
            SlotSpec("order_id",   required=True,  filled=True),
            SlotSpec("issue_type", required=True,  filled=True),
            SlotSpec("resolution", required=True,  filled=False),
        ],
        confidence_min=0.0,  # confidence has already failed
        memory_scope="session",
    ),
    fail_path=FailPath(
        trigger="No agent available",
        response="Offer callback with full conversation transcript emailed to user",
        max_turns=0,  # immediate escalation, no retry
    ),
)
Job stories for conversational flows should be organised into a story map that covers every turn state for every major task. The table below shows the minimum coverage for a customer-service chatbot.
Each cell represents a story that must exist before a task flow is considered fully specified. Incomplete coverage is a readiness risk, not a gap to fill during QA.
Generating Acceptance Criteria from Job Stories
Job stories become actionable only when they produce machine-checkable acceptance criteria. The conversion is mechanical given the five-element structure.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ConversationalAC:
    given: str
    when:  str
    then:  str
    and_not: str = ""  # negative assertion (important for correction stories)
    story_id: str = ""

    def gherkin(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"GIVEN {self.given}",
            f"WHEN  {self.when}",
            f"THEN  {self.then}",
        ]
        if self.and_not:
            lines.append(f"AND   NOT {self.and_not}")
        if self.story_id:
            lines.append(f"# Story: {self.story_id}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

def acs_from_job_story(story: "ConversationalJobStory") -> list[ConversationalAC]:
    acs = []
    state = story.ai_context.turn_state

    if story.requires_clarification():
        missing = story.ai_context.unfilled_required_slots()
        acs.append(ConversationalAC(
            given=f"the conversation is in state {state.value} with {missing} unfilled",
            when=f"the user says: '{story.situation}'",
            then="the system asks exactly one clarifying question targeting the first missing slot",
            story_id=story.story_id,
        ))

    if state == TurnState.CORRECTION:
        preserved = [s.name for s in story.ai_context.slots
                     if s.filled and s.name not in ["order_id"]]
        acs.append(ConversationalAC(
            given=f"the conversation is in {state.value} state with prior context",
            when="the user provides a corrected value for a previously filled slot",
            then="only the corrected slot is updated in dialog state",
            and_not=f"any of {preserved} are reset or re-asked",
            story_id=story.story_id,
        ))

    if state == TurnState.ESCALATION:
        acs.append(ConversationalAC(
            given="the AI has exceeded max_turns without resolving the user's goal",
            when="the escalation threshold is reached",
            then="a handoff is offered with a conversation summary pre-populated",
            story_id=story.story_id,
        ))

    # Fail-path AC always generated
    acs.append(ConversationalAC(
        given=f"the condition: {story.fail_path.trigger}",
        when="the AI attempts to fulfil the story outcome",
        then=story.fail_path.response,
        story_id=f"{story.story_id}-FAIL",
    ))

    return acs


# Example: generate ACs for the correction story
for ac in acs_from_job_story(CORRECTION_STORY):
    print(ac.gherkin())
    print()
Integrating Job Stories with Conversation Design Tools
Job stories should flow into conversation design artefacts, not exist as isolated documents. The integration points below prevent stories from becoming stale.
Run the coverage report in CI as part of the Definition of Ready check. A story backlog with
below 1.0 or untested nodes in the dialogue flow should block the sprint from starting.
Job stories for conversational AI extend the standard WHEN / I WANT TO / SO I CAN format with two mandatory elements:
(turn state, slot contents, confidence requirements) and
(explicit degradation path). This produces stories that cover the full conversational lifecycle rather than only the happy-path flow.
Write at least one story per turn state (Entry, Mid, Correction, Escalation, Session-End) for every major task flow.
Use the five-element structure to generate Gherkin acceptance criteria programmatically — the conversion is mechanical and prevents interpretation drift.
Persist stories in the same repository as the codebase and link them to dialogue-flow nodes so coverage gaps surface in CI.
A correction story's negative assertion (only the corrected slot resets) is the most commonly missing test in conversational AI flows.
Escalation stories must specify the handoff package content, not just the trigger — agents receiving context-free handoffs recreate the user's frustration.
Teams that author job stories before building conversational flows ship fewer confusion-repair bugs and produce dialogue designs that remain coherent as turn counts grow.
